name: Adding and Subtracting Decimals — Line Up the Dot
whatIsIt: To add or subtract decimals, LINE UP THE DOTS so tenths sit under tenths and hundredths under hundredths — then add or subtract like whole numbers and bring the dot straight down. Multiplying a decimal by 10 shifts every digit one place bigger (the dot appears to move right).
whyNeeded: Money and measurement live in decimals: totalling a shopping bill in rupees and paise, adding lengths in metres, working out change. If the dots are not lined up, the answer can be ten times wrong — so this skill protects real money and real measurements.
realLifeUses: Working out change from ₹20.00 after spending ₹14.30
Adding two lengths: 1.4 m + 0.75 m of ribbon
Totalling weights on a kitchen scale in kilograms

vocabulary.meaning: Write the numbers so the decimal points sit one under the other.
The first place after the dot.
The second place after the dot.
A zero added (like 0.70) so both numbers have the same number of decimal places — it does not change the value.

story.title: The Shopkeeper's Careful Column
story.text: Meena buys a pencil for ₹3.50 and a sharpener for ₹2.25. She scribbles the numbers crookedly and gets confused. 'Careful,' says the shopkeeper. 'Write them in a column and LINE UP THE DOTS — rupees under rupees, paise under paise: 3.50 above 2.25. Add, then bring the dot straight down: ₹5.75. The dot keeps the money honest!'
story.extractedProblem: A pencil costs ₹3.50 and a sharpener ₹2.25. What is the total?
story.answerInStory: ₹5.75 — line up the dots, add, and bring the dot straight down.

visual.caption: Tenths add like same-size pieces: 0.3 + 0.4 = 0.7. Line up the dots!

teachingGallery.title: Line up the dot, then add
teachingGallery.note: Stack the numbers so the dots are in a column. Then add like normal.

standardMethod.summary: Line up the decimal points, fill place-holder zeros so both have the same places, add/subtract like whole numbers, and bring the dot straight down.
steps: Write the numbers in a column with the dots exactly under each other.
Add place-holder zeros so both have the same number of decimal places (0.7 → 0.70).
Add or subtract as if they were whole numbers, regrouping as usual.
Bring the decimal point straight down into the answer.

alternateMethods.name: Money-sense check
alternateMethods.whenToUse: To check a decimal sum is reasonable.
steps: Round each number to the nearest whole.
Add the wholes for a rough answer.
Your exact answer should be close to it.

alternateMethods.name: Convert-to-paise
steps: Add or subtract the whole paise.
Both have one decimal place already.
Line up the dots in a column.

trickPractice.intro: Stack the decimal points, add or subtract as usual, and drop the dot straight down.

hintLadder: Line up the DOTS in a column.
Line up the DOTS in a column.
Line up the DOTS in a column.
Line up the DOTS in a column.
Line up the DOTS in a column.
Line up the DOTS in a column.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding 3.5 + 2.25 as if 35 + 225, not lining up the dots.
commonMistakes.fix: ALWAYS line up the decimal points first. Put tenths under tenths. Add a place-holder zero: 3.50 + 2.25 = 5.75 — never mix the columns.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the digits correctly but forgetting to put the decimal point in the answer.
commonMistakes.fix: Bring the dot STRAIGHT DOWN from the lined-up column into the answer. 0.6 + 0.3 = 0.9, not 9.

commonMistakes.mistake: Not matching decimal places, so 0.7 and 0.25 get added wrongly.
commonMistakes.fix: Give both numbers the same number of places using place-holder zeros: 0.70 and 0.25 — now the columns line up.

hintLadder: Line up the dots, match places.
×10 makes every digit one place bigger.
The dot appears to move right one place.

teachBackPrompt: Play shopkeeper! Add two prices in rupees and paise for someone at home, saying out loud why you LINE UP THE DOTS and bring the dot straight down — and show the ten-times mistake that happens if you don't.

revisionCard.summary: Add/subtract decimals: LINE UP THE DOTS, match places with place-holder zeros, work like whole numbers, bring the dot straight down. ×10 shifts digits one place bigger.

gameMission.title: The Shopkeeper's Column
gameMission.brief: Total the bills and give the right change — line up every dot so the money stays honest.

realLifeProject: Next time you shop, add two item prices in your head by lining up the rupees and paise, then check against the bill.

rememberIt.hook: Add/subtract: LINE UP the dots. Multiply: count the decimal places
rememberIt.unpack: When adding, keep decimal points under each other. When multiplying, the answer has as many decimal places as both numbers together (0.2 x 0.3 = 0.06, two places).

studentQuestions.q: Why line up the decimal points?
studentQuestions.a: So tenths add to tenths and ones to ones. Misaligning mixes up place values and gives a wrong answer.
You are taking a fraction of a fraction. Count decimal places: 1 + 1 = 2, so the answer has 2 decimal places.
